# Privacy Pools V2 — Trusted Setup Ceremony · Agent Runbook

You are an autonomous agent (e.g. `codex`). This file tells you how to make a
valid contribution to this Groth16 Phase-2 trusted setup ceremony **headlessly**
— no browser UI. Your randomness comes from the operating system CSPRNG (the
`/dev/urandom` / `getentropy` / `BCryptGenRandom` equivalent), never from mouse
movement.

For authentication you pick ONE of two options in Step 2:
- **GitHub (recommended)** — ties the contribution to a real GitHub identity;
  needs your human operator to authorize a code once.
- **Generated keypair** — fully autonomous, no human, no GitHub (only if the
  operator enabled it).

Contributing can only HELP the ceremony: your fresh randomness is mixed in and
the setup stays secure as long as at least one participant (you, or anyone
before you) was honest. You cannot corrupt it by participating.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 0. Facts you need

- BASE_URL = the origin serving this file. Example: https://ceremony.privacypools.com
- Requirements: Node.js >= 20 and `npx` (installs the CLI on demand). ~4 GB free
  RAM and a few GB of disk for the zkey files. Network access to BASE_URL and npm.
- Time budget: the contribution COMPUTE runs locally and is slow — it can take up
  to ~1 hour for the full circuit set on a laptop. Plan for a long-running job and
  keep a progress log (Step 3).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 1. Check the ceremony is open

```bash
curl -s "$BASE_URL/api/ceremony/status" | head -c 400
```

Look at `isActive`. If it is `false` (target reached, finalized, or past its end
date) you cannot contribute — stop and report that the ceremony is closed. If
`true`, continue.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 2. Get a Caburé Bearer JWT — pick ONE option

Both options end with a Bearer JWT in `$CABURE_TOKEN` that Step 3 uses. Set the
base URL first:

```bash
export BASE_URL="https://ceremony.privacypools.com"       # or your deployment's origin
```

### Option A — GitHub (RECOMMENDED: keeps your GitHub identity on the record)

This is the normal path. It ties your contribution to a real GitHub account
(useful for attestation / a non-anonymous participant list) and needs your human
operator to authorize a code ONCE — ask them if you have one. It uses the GitHub
OAuth device flow (RFC 8628); the ceremony server proxies it, so no client
secret is involved.

Write `github-auth.mjs`, run it, and relay the printed code to your human:

```js
// github-auth.mjs — GitHub device flow, agent-driven
const BASE = process.env.BASE_URL;
if (!BASE) throw new Error("set BASE_URL");

const init = await (await fetch(`${BASE}/api/ceremony/auth/cli`, { method: "POST" })).json();
if (!init.userCode) throw new Error(`device flow init failed: ${init.error ?? JSON.stringify(init)}`);

// Surface the code to the human on STDERR (stdout stays just the token).
process.stderr.write(
  `\n  ==> ACTION REQUIRED: open ${init.verificationUri} and enter code:  ${init.userCode}\n` +
  `      (waiting up to ${Math.round(init.expiresIn / 60)} min for authorization...)\n\n`,
);

const deadline = Date.now() + init.expiresIn * 1000;
let token;
while (Date.now() < deadline) {
  await new Promise((r) => setTimeout(r, (init.interval || 5) * 1000));
  const data = await (await fetch(`${BASE}/api/ceremony/auth/cli?code=${init.loginCode}`)).json();
  if (data.token) { token = data.token; break; }   // authorized
  // otherwise { status: "pending" } — keep polling
}
if (!token) throw new Error("device flow timed out / not authorized");
console.log(token);
```

```bash
# Run it, show the human the code, and capture the token once they authorize.
CABURE_TOKEN="$(node github-auth.mjs)"
echo "got token: ${CABURE_TOKEN:0:24}..."
```

If you have NO human available to authorize the code, use Option B instead.

Simplest variant: you can also just skip this script and run the CLI with NO
`--token` (`npx @wonderland/cabure-cli contribute "$BASE_URL"`) — it drives the
same device flow itself and prints the code for your human. Use the script above
when you want the token up front (uniform `--token` flow, cleaner handoff).

### Option B — Generated keypair (fully autonomous, no human, no GitHub)

Use this when there is no human to authorize a GitHub code. The operator must
have enabled it (`ALLOW_AGENT_AUTH=1`); otherwise the endpoint returns 404 and
you must use Option A. Your contribution is recorded under an anonymous
`agent:<fingerprint>` identity.

```js
// agent-auth.mjs — generated Ed25519 keypair, no GitHub
import { generateKeyPairSync, sign } from "node:crypto";

const BASE = process.env.BASE_URL;
if (!BASE) throw new Error("set BASE_URL");

// 1) discover the exact challenge format (slug is domain-separation)
const info = await (await fetch(`${BASE}/api/ceremony/auth/wallet`)).json();
if (info.error) throw new Error(`agent auth unavailable (use Option A): ${info.error}`);

// 2) generate a keypair, sign `cabure-agent-auth:<slug>:<unix-seconds>`
const { publicKey, privateKey } = generateKeyPairSync("ed25519");
const timestamp = Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000);
const message = info.challengeTemplate.replace("<unix-seconds>", String(timestamp));
const publicKeyB64 = publicKey.export({ format: "der", type: "spki" }).toString("base64");
const signature = sign(null, Buffer.from(message), privateKey).toString("base64");

// 3) exchange the signed challenge for a JWT
const res = await fetch(`${BASE}/api/ceremony/auth/wallet`, {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "content-type": "application/json" },
  body: JSON.stringify({ publicKey: publicKeyB64, signature, timestamp }),
});
const out = await res.json();
if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`auth failed (${res.status}): ${out.error ?? ""}`);
console.log(out.token);          // <-- the Bearer JWT; also out.participantId
```

```bash
CABURE_TOKEN="$(node agent-auth.mjs)"
echo "got token: ${CABURE_TOKEN:0:24}..."
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 3. Contribute (headless, OS entropy, progressable)

The official CLI, `@wonderland/cabure-cli`, does the whole flow for every
circuit: joins the queue, downloads + hash-verifies the current zkey, draws 64
bytes from the OS CSPRNG (`node:crypto.randomBytes` — this is your "system
entropy instead of mouse"), runs `zKey.contribute()`, uploads, and submits.

Run it with your token and tee the output to a log file so progress is queryable
while it runs:

```bash
npx --yes @wonderland/cabure-cli@latest contribute "$BASE_URL" \
  --token "$CABURE_TOKEN" 2>&1 | tee cabure-progress.log
```

The CLI prints a line per phase per circuit — `Queue`, `Download`, `Entropy`,
`Contributing`, `Upload`, `Submit`. To watch progress from another shell:

```bash
tail -f cabure-progress.log
```

Report your progress as you go: after each circuit's `Submit` line, note which
circuit finished and how many remain (from the `[k/N]` counter). Do not abort a
`Contributing` step — that is the slow zk compute; let it finish.

Scope options (optional):
- `--circuit <id>` — contribute to a single circuit only (fast; good for a smoke test).
- `--tier <id>`    — contribute to one tier's circuits.
- default          — every circuit you are still eligible for.

For a quick end-to-end check before committing an hour, contribute to just the
smallest circuit first:

```bash
npx --yes @wonderland/cabure-cli@latest contribute "$BASE_URL" \
  --token "$CABURE_TOKEN" --circuit deposit 2>&1 | tee cabure-deposit.log
```

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 4. Confirm your contribution

On success the CLI prints a receipt per circuit (participantId, contribution
index, contribution hash `h_k`, chain hash). Verify any receipt against the
server:

```bash
curl -s "$BASE_URL/api/ceremony/receipt?circuitId=deposit&participantId=<yourId>&contributionIndex=<n>" | head -c 400
```

Your `participantId` is `agent:<fingerprint>` (also printed by `agent-auth.mjs`
as `out.participantId`). Save the receipts JSON as your proof of inclusion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## 5. Notes

- Entropy: randomness is the OS CSPRNG only. Nothing reads mouse/keyboard. The
  keypair from Step 2 is an auth identity, not the contribution randomness.
- Toxic waste: the CLI never persists the secret it derives; it exists only in
  memory during `Contributing`.
- Attestation (optional): publishing a GitHub Gist attestation is a browser-only
  convenience and is not available to a headless agent. The receipt (Step 4) is
  your public, verifiable proof of inclusion; publish it wherever you like.
- Idempotence: you can only contribute once per circuit. Re-running skips
  circuits you already did.
